text: In a CI/CD pipeline, at which stage are configuration tests MOST typically executed?

options.text: After deployment to the production environment.
As the first quality gate in the pipeline, checking file paths and environment configuration before building.
Only in the preproduction environment as part of non-functional testing.
Manually by the infrastructure team before the pipeline starts.

explanation: Configuration tests verify that file paths, environment variables, and configuration files are correct before the build begins. They run early in the pipeline as a fast quality gate to catch environmental setup issues before investing time in building and deploying. Running them after production deployment would be too late to be cost-effective.

text: A team is deciding how to integrate system-level automated tests into their pipeline. They choose to run them as a separate pipeline triggered AFTER deployment, rather than as part of the deployment phase itself. What is a KEY DISADVANTAGE of this approach?

options.text: It does not allow flexible test suite selection.
The tests are not a quality gate — a failed test cannot automatically prevent the deployment from completing.
Tests must always be run sequentially with no parallelism.
Separate pipelines cannot generate test reports.

explanation: When system-level tests run in a separate pipeline triggered after deployment, they offer flexibility in test suite selection but lose the ability to act as a deployment quality gate. If tests fail, the deployment has already completed, so a rollback (if needed) requires a separate redeployment. The approach that includes tests within the deployment phase allows for automatic rollback on failure.

text: Where should test environment configuration data (such as URLs and credentials) be stored to ensure the test automation framework can access the correct configuration for each environment?

options.text: Hardcoded directly in the test scripts to ensure they always use the correct values.
Stored with the testware in version control, with environment-specific values managed as configuration.
Only in the testers' personal notes, to prevent accidental exposure.
In a single global file shared across all pipelines without environment-specific separation.

explanation: Test environment configuration data (URLs, credentials, environment-specific settings) should be stored with the testware in version control, with environment-specific values clearly separated (e.g., via configuration files, environment variables, or secrets management). This ensures reproducibility and traceability. Hardcoding in scripts is an anti-pattern; storing only in personal notes prevents team access and reproducibility.

text: A team uses feature toggles in their release management strategy. What is the PRIMARY purpose of feature toggles in the context of test automation?

options.text: They allow tests to be permanently disabled without deleting them from the codebase.
They enable certain features to be enabled or disabled per release, allowing testware to be configured to match the feature state being tested.
They automatically generate test cases for new features.
They replace the need for separate test environments.

explanation: Feature toggles (feature flags) allow features to be switched on or off per release or environment. In test automation, this means testware can be configured to match the feature state of the SUT being tested — enabling tests for features that are active and excluding tests for features that are toggled off. This supports continuous delivery without needing separate branches for each feature.

text: What is the PRIMARY purpose of contract testing in a microservices or API-based architecture?

options.text: To verify that the UI of each microservice matches the design specification.
To verify that services can communicate with each other and that the shared data structures are consistent between consumer and provider.
To measure the performance of API endpoints under load.
To check that API documentation is published in the correct format.

explanation: Contract testing verifies that services (consumers and providers) can communicate correctly and that the data exchanged between them is consistent with agreed contracts. It allows defects in service integrations to be found early in the SDLC, before full integration testing. It is distinct from UI testing, performance testing, and documentation validation.

text: In consumer-driven contract testing, who is responsible for defining the expectations (the contract)?

options.text: The infrastructure team defines the contract based on network capabilities.
The consumer service defines the contract by specifying the interactions it expects from the provider.
The provider service defines the contract and the consumer must conform to it.
An independent third-party arbitrator defines the contract.

explanation: In consumer-driven contract testing, the consuming service defines the contract — it specifies the interactions (requests and expected responses) it requires from the provider. The provider then verifies that it can fulfil those expectations. This is distinct from provider-driven contract testing, where the provider defines the contract. Consumer-driven contracts help catch breaking changes to provider APIs before deployment.

text: At which stage of the CI/CD pipeline are component integration tests MOST appropriately placed?

options.text: In the CI pipeline, after component tests pass, as part of the build phase quality gate.
Only in the production environment after a canary release.
As the final step before merging into the main branch, after system tests.
In a completely separate manual testing phase outside the pipeline.

explanation: Component integration tests verify that individual components interact correctly. They are placed in the CI pipeline after component tests pass, acting as part of the build quality gate. They run in the build environment without requiring full deployment. System tests come later in the delivery pipeline, after deployment to the integration environment.

text: When documenting API infrastructure for test automation purposes, which information is MOST critical to capture?

options.text: The colour scheme of the API documentation portal.
API parameters, request/response types, headers, and the relationships between business APIs.
The name of the developer who originally wrote each API endpoint.
The estimated number of users who will call each API endpoint per year.

explanation: For test automation of API-based systems, critical documentation includes API parameters (required and optional), expected request and response types, authentication headers, and the relationships between APIs (which APIs depend on or call other APIs). This information is essential for building effective API test automation and understanding service dependencies.

text: Which type of automated tests is MOST appropriate for inclusion in a nightly regression pipeline in addition to the standard deployment pipeline?

options.text: Only configuration tests, as they run fastest.
Long-running regression suites and non-functional tests that are too time-consuming for the main deployment pipeline.
No automated tests should be run nightly — only on-demand.
Exclusively manual test checklists converted to pipeline scripts.

explanation: Nightly regression pipelines are designed to run longer, more comprehensive test suites and non-functional tests (e.g., performance, security) that take too long to include in the main deployment pipeline. These tests provide deeper quality feedback while not blocking the rapid deployment cycle. Configuration tests are fast enough for the main pipeline.

text: There is an IT company which develops an often-changing financial software product using the Agile software development model. The development, integration and deployment processes are highly automated with an established CI/CD pipeline. You are working on a TAS with the goal of creating a TAS which can be used for as many test automation purposes as possible. Which of the following options are valid purposes for test automation in this case?

options.text: Run a regression test suite every night
Execute a build of a component
Run a static code analysis
Execute an automated performance efficiency test in the CI/CD pipeline
Package and deploy the application as part of the deployment phase

explanation: Regression testing is necessary and can be incorporated into the CI/CD pipeline. Performance efficiency tests can be automated and run in the CI/CD pipeline. Executing a build is not a test automation task. Running static code analysis is not a test automation task. Packaging and deployment are not test automation tasks.

text: Which statement is correct regarding automated test execution in a CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: Tests are not executed as part of the deployment phase.
Tests are not executed as a separate pipeline triggered by the successful deployment.
Test cases do not act as a quality gate when different automated test suites will run on each deployment.
Pipelines are not recommended for regression testing due to the extensive scope and size of these tests.

explanation: When automated test suites run as separate pipelines triggered after deployment, they do not act as quality gates — a failed test cannot prevent the deployment from completing. Tests are executed during deployment (A is wrong). Tests can be triggered to execute after successful deployment as a separate pipeline (B is wrong). Pipelines are well-suited for regression testing regardless of size (D is wrong).

text: How is configuration management used in test automation?

options.text: It enables the management of test data and test environment configurations.
The SUT configuration can be stored and easily removed.
It enables management of user rights for accessing test automation.
Test automation results can easily be analyzed.

explanation: Configuration management in test automation enables the management of test data and test environment configurations such as URLs, credentials, and environment-specific settings. SUT configuration can be under configuration management but is not directly related to test automation. User rights management is not related to configuration management. Configuration management does not support test automation results analysis.

text: Which item below is NOT part of the test environment configuration?

options.text: Uniform resource locators (URLs)
Credentials
Test data
Common core library

explanation: The common core library is not part of the test environment configuration. The test environment configuration is actually a part of the common core library, not vice versa. URLs, credentials, and test data are all part of the test environment configuration.

text: How does contract testing NOT contribute to API test automation dependencies in an infrastructure?

options.text: Ensures that APIs adhere to predefined contracts for communication.
Can be used to test the communication of microservices.
Validates the compatibility of two separate systems.
Verifies whether a system satisfies its contractual requirements.

explanation: Contract testing has no relation to verifying whether a system satisfies its contractual (legal/SLA) requirements — that refers to business contracts, not technical API contracts. Contract testing ensures APIs follow predefined communication agreements, can be used to test communication between microservices, and validates the compatibility of two separate systems.

text: You are on a project where the teams are working on breaking down an old monolithic web service into several microservices. Which of the following documents can assist you in identifying dependencies and developing your Test Automation Solution (TAS) for API testing? Consider: i. Application programming interface (API) specification. ii. System architecture diagram. iii. Test strategy. iv. Release notes.

options.text: i, ii, and iv
i and ii
ii, iii, and iv
i only

explanation: The API specification provides details about endpoints, and the system architecture diagram shows the relationships between different components. Together they provide the necessary information to identify API dependencies. Release notes do not help learn API connections and details. The test strategy and release notes don't provide specific API dependency information needed for building a TAS. The API specification alone is insufficient without the architecture diagram.

text: Your organization is using a modern continuous integration system which differentiates between the build and deployment phases of the continuous delivery pipelines. The developers have implemented some testing in the build pipeline and that seems to be working, but you are seeing frequent issues with transaction workflows that are either missing or not implemented correctly. You have now developed tests to cover the problem scenarios. How should this testing be implemented in the pipeline?

options.text: It should be implemented as integration tests in the continuous integration pipeline
It should be implemented as component integration tests in the continuous deployment pipeline
It should be implemented as system tests in the continuous deployment pipeline
It should be implemented as component tests in the continuous integration pipeline

explanation: Transaction workflow tests are system-level tests and should be implemented in the continuous deployment pipeline. System tests require a fully deployed and integrated environment, which is available in the deployment pipeline but not the build pipeline.

text: You are developing performance efficiency tests to combat production issues with slow performance, particularly with any reporting transactions. Where should these be implemented in a CI/CD environment to provide the earliest quality gate?

options.text: In the continuous integration pipeline
In the continuous deployment pipeline
After the deployment, in the test environment
After the deployment, in the production environment

explanation: Implementing performance efficiency tests in the continuous deployment pipeline would give the earliest feasible feedback. The CI pipeline is not correct because reports will not be executable this early in the build cycle. Running after deployment in a test or production environment is later than necessary.

text: How are test environments influenced by configuration management?

options.text: Environments are only accessed through the configuration management software
Environments are secured and security tested when added to configuration management
Environment configuration and access information can be stored in configuration management
Environment access is controlled by the configuration management system and software

explanation: In the case of multiple environments being used, the configuration and URLs of specific environments can be tracked under configuration management to ensure changes are recorded and versions are clearly saved and reusable.

text: Why is it important to understand the API connections?

options.text: Developers will be coding according to these requirements
Performance testing is dependent on understanding each API connection
This is where the tester will find the documentation regarding the required parameters
The business logic is controlled through the API connections and their relationships

explanation: Test automation should ensure it is automating realistic transactions, and this requires understanding the business logic that underlies the various APIs and how they relate to each other. The business logic is controlled through API connections and their relationships.

text: What is the purpose of a contract between services?

options.text: It defines how the provider will supply services to the consumer
It defines who has access to which data for security purposes
It defines how a service signs on and off the system
It defines the expected speed of transactions and allows that to be tuned up or down as needed

explanation: A contract between services defines how the provider will supply services to the consumer, specifying the expected request and response formats, data types, and interaction patterns. This enables both services to be tested independently against the contract.

text: Automated regression test scripts make calls to private APIs requiring authentication (same method for all APIs) on a business-critical SUT. Two changes are planned: a change to the API authentication method and a minor OS upgrade in the test environment. You have already updated the test scripts for the new authentication method. Which sequence of activities is BEST to ensure the test scripts are not adversely affected?

options.text: First upgrade the OS, then implement the authentication change, then run all updated test scripts.
Implement one change at a time and run a subset of updated test scripts after each change, finally running all updated test scripts.
First implement the authentication change, then upgrade the OS, then run all updated test scripts.
Implement one change at a time and run a subset of updated test scripts after each change.

explanation: When multiple changes are introduced, applying them all before testing makes it harder to attribute failures to a specific change. The best practice is to implement changes incrementally, validating with a representative subset of tests after each change to detect issues early — especially important for a business-critical system. After each change is validated, the full updated regression suite should still be run to ensure overall coverage. Applying two changes before testing (A, C) reduces diagnostic clarity; stopping after subset validation without a final full run (D) omits the broad coverage check needed after all changes are applied.

text: Which of the following does NOT refer to a configuration item that should be specified in development pipelines to identify a test environment (and its specific test data) for a web app under test?

options.text: The number and type of automated tests to execute in the test environment where the web app is deployed.
The base URL of the test environment where the web app is deployed.
The connection string(s) to connect to the test database(s) within the test environment.
The URLs of web APIs/services related to the web app's backend within the test environment.

explanation: Configuration items that identify a specific test environment and its data are those that define where the SUT runs and how automation connects to it and its dependencies: base URL, backend service endpoints, and database connection strings. The number and type of automated tests to execute is a test selection/orchestration decision (what to run), not an environment identification configuration (where to run) — the same environment identity supports running different test subsets.

text: You are adding build verification (smoke) tests to a CI/CD pipeline for an Agile project, to verify daily build stability and ensure recent changes have not broken core functionality. Static source code analysis is currently the pipeline's first activity. At which stage should you add these smoke tests?

options.text: As a first activity, before static analysis and before generating the new build.
After static analysis and before generating the new build.
After deploying the new build to the test environment and before more extensive testing.
As a final activity, immediately before releasing the new build into production.

explanation: Smoke tests provide fast confirmation that a build is deployable and core end-to-end functionality remains intact, so they need a running instance of the SUT — they must run after deployment to a test environment. Running them before generating the build (A, B) is infeasible since there is no deployed artifact yet. Running them only immediately before production release (D) defeats their purpose as an early feedback mechanism. The correct placement is immediately after deployment to the test environment and before broader, longer-running suites.

text: A CI/CD pipeline has a build phase (running Component Testing and Component Integration Testing) and a deployment phase, which provisions infrastructure, deploys the SUT, then triggers a separate pipeline running System Testing and Acceptance Testing. Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: Both automated test cases for CT-CIT and ST-AT can act as quality gates.
Automated test cases for CT-CIT can act as quality gates, while automated test cases for ST-AT cannot.
Automated test cases for CT-CIT cannot act as quality gates, while automated test cases for ST-AT can.
Neither automated test cases for CT-CIT nor for ST-AT can act as quality gates.

explanation: Quality gates are checkpoints where objective criteria determine whether a pipeline may proceed. In the build phase, CT and CIT commonly act as fast quality gates, blocking promotion on failure. After provisioning and deployment, automated System Testing and Acceptance Testing can also act as gates for promoting a build to later stages, provided downstream promotion depends on their outcome — which is the case here even though ST/AT run in a separately triggered pipeline. Both sets of automated tests can therefore act as quality gates.

text: A new TAS supports data-driven test scripts. All tasks for its initial deployment — installing/configuring TAS components and provisioning infrastructure — will be performed manually by a dedicated, specialised team. The TAS is expected to be deployed in other similar environments in the future. You see a risk that correct, reproducible deployment of the TAS cannot be guaranteed. Which option is BEST suited to mitigate this risk?

options.text: Nothing needs to be done, because the specialized team will not make mistakes and will ensure correct, reproducible deployment.
Partition the data tables used by data-driven scripts into smaller tables using an appropriate logical criterion, to make them more manageable.
Review data-driven test scripts to better organize test libraries by adding functions for identical action sequences common across scripts.
Automate most of the tasks related to installing/configuring the TAS components and provisioning the infrastructure.

explanation: Repeatable, reliable deployment of the TAS is foundational, especially when it will be rolled out to multiple environments. Manual installation and provisioning are error-prone and hard to reproduce consistently even with skilled teams, due to small variations and configuration drift. Automating deployment (scripted installation, configuration management, infrastructure as code) supports traceability, repeatability, and rapid recovery. Human processes are never guaranteed error-free (making A unsafe), and reorganising test data or libraries (B, C) improves test maintainability but does not address non-reproducible TAS deployment.
